preamble.summary: A psalm of refuge and praise, attributed to David when he fled from Saul into a cave. David takes shelter under God's wings, declares his trust amid enemies whose teeth are spears and whose tongues are sharp swords, and erupts into praise that aims to wake the dawn. The psalm features the refrain 'Be exalted above the heavens, God; let your glory be over all the earth.'
preamble.remarkable: The psalm's most striking moment is verse 9 (Hebrew): 'Wake up, my glory! Wake up, harp and lyre! I will wake the dawn.' David does not wait for the dawn to come and bring hope — he commands his instruments to rouse the dawn itself. This is the audacity of worship in darkness: the singer does not wait for conditions to improve before praising. The praise itself becomes the agent that summons the new day. The cave setting (whether Adullam, 1 Samuel 22:1, or En-gedi, 1 Samuel 24:3) adds physical texture — David is literally underground, in darkness, hiding, and from that place he declares that God's faithfulness reaches the skies (v. 11).
preamble.friction: The superscription says al tashchet ('do not destroy'), which may be a melody name or a liturgical instruction. Some connect it to David's refusal to destroy Saul in the cave at En-gedi (1 Samuel 24:4-7) or in the Wilderness of Ziph (1 Samuel 26:9). The second half of this psalm (vv. 8-12) reappears almost identically as Psalm 108:2-6, suggesting that psalm compositions could be modular — sections recombined for different liturgical purposes.
preamble.connections: The cave setting connects to 1 Samuel 22:1 (cave of Adullam) or 24:1-7 (cave at En-gedi). The 'wings' refuge image (v. 2) echoes Ruth 2:12 and Psalm 36:7. The teeth-as-spears imagery (v. 5) parallels Psalm 52:4 and Proverbs 30:14. Psalm 108:2-6 reuses verses 8-12. The faithfulness-to-the-skies language (v. 11) appears also in Psalm 36:5 and 89:2.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֣חַ אַל־תַּ֭שְׁחֵת לְדָוִ֣ד מִכְתָּ֑ם בְּבׇרְח֖וֹ מִפְּנֵי־שָׁא֣וּל בַּמְּעָרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, Altaschith, Michtam of David, when he fled from Saul in the cave.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. "Do Not Destroy."
A miktam of David, when he fled from Saul into the cave.
translator_notes: al tashchet ('do not destroy') appears in four psalm superscriptions (57, 58, 59, 75). Whether this is a melody title, a liturgical instruction, or a thematic label is uncertain. If connected to the cave narrative, it may recall David's command to his men not to destroy Saul when they had the chance (1 Samuel 24:6; 26:9). The miktam designation links this psalm to the cluster of Psalms 56-60.

verses.text_hebrew: חׇנֵּ֤נִי אֱלֹהִ֨ים ׀ חׇנֵּ֗נִי כִּ֥י בְךָ֮ חָסָ֢יָה נַ֫פְשִׁ֥י וּבְצֵל־כְּנָפֶ֥יךָ אֶחְסֶ֑ה עַ֝֗ד יַעֲבֹ֥ר הַוּֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Be merciful unto me, O God, be merciful unto me: for my soul trusteth in thee: yea, in the shadow of thy wings will I make my refuge, until these calamities be overpast.
verses.rendering:
Be gracious to me, God — be gracious to me,
for in you my soul takes refuge.
In the shadow of your wings I will take shelter
until the storms of destruction pass.
verses.expanded_rendering: The doubled chonneni ('be gracious to me, be gracious to me') is rare in the Psalter and signals extreme urgency. David's nefesh ('soul, life, entire being') chasayah ('takes refuge') in God — the verb is from chasah, the act of sheltering under something stronger than yourself. The image then materializes: be-tsel kenafekha ('in the shadow of your wings'). This is the mother-bird image — the hen spreading wings over chicks during a storm (cf. Ruth 2:12, Matthew 23:37). David, a warrior and king, pictures himself as a chick under God's wing. He will shelter there ad ya'avor havvot ('until the storms of destruction pass over'). The havvot ('destructions, calamities') are temporary weather; God's wings are permanent shelter.
translator_notes: The cave provides a physical analog for the theological image: David is literally hiding in a dark enclosed space while enemies search for him outside. The 'wings' transform the cave from a hole in the ground to the shelter of God's presence.
havvot ('destructions') is the same word used in Psalm 52:4 for what the wicked tongue devises. David is sheltering from the same destructive forces that the previous psalms have been naming.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶ֭קְרָא לֵאלֹהִ֣ים עֶלְי֑וֹן לָ֝אֵ֗ל גֹּמֵ֥ר עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will cry unto God most high; unto God that performeth all things for me.
verses.rendering:
I call out to God Most High,
to God who fulfills his purpose for me.
translator_notes: gomer alai ('who completes/finishes concerning me') — God is not a partial actor but one who carries things to completion. The verb gamar means 'to finish, to complete, to bring to an end.' Whatever God has started in David's life, he will see it through. This anticipates Paul's confidence in Philippians 1:6.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׁלַ֤ח מִשָּׁמַ֨יִם ׀ וְיוֹשִׁ֘יעֵ֤נִי חֵרֵ֣ף שֹׁאֲפִ֣י סֶ֑לָה יִשְׁלַ֥ח אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים חַסְדּ֥וֹ וַאֲמִתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall send from heaven, and save me from the reproach of him that would swallow me up. Selah. God shall send forth his mercy and his truth.
verses.rendering:
He will send from heaven and save me;
he rebukes the one who tramples me.
Selah.
God will send his faithful love and his truth.
verses.expanded_rendering: God's rescue comes mi-shamayim ('from heaven') — the help descends vertically into the horizontal crisis. What God sends has two names: chasdo ('his faithful love') and amitto ('his truth/faithfulness'). chesed and emet are paired throughout the Hebrew Bible as God's two defining covenant attributes — loyal love that will not let go and reliable truth that does not waver. These are not abstract qualities but active agents: God sends them the way a king dispatches envoys. chesed and emet arrive on the scene and accomplish the rescue.
translator_notes: The pairing of chesed ve-emet ('faithful love and truth') is one of the most common divine attribute pairs in the Hebrew Bible (Genesis 24:27, Psalm 25:10, 85:10, 89:14). The two qualities are inseparable: love without truth is sentimentality; truth without love is brutality. God's character holds both in perfect union.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love and reliability, covenant faithfulness and trustworthiness
key_terms.note: This word pair functions as a compound description of God's covenant character. When God sends chesed ve-emet, he sends his own nature into the situation. The pair appears at key moments of divine intervention throughout the Hebrew Bible.

verses.text_hebrew: נַפְשִׁ֤י ׀ בְּת֥וֹךְ לְבָאִם֮ אֶשְׁכְּבָ֢ה לֹ֫הֲטִ֥ים בְּֽנֵי־אָדָ֗ם שִׁ֭נֵּיהֶם חֲנִ֣ית וְחִצִּ֑ים וּ֝לְשׁוֹנָ֗ם חֶ֣רֶב חַדָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: My soul is among lions: and I lie even among them that are set on fire, even the sons of men, whose teeth are spears and arrows, and their tongue a sharp sword.
verses.rendering:
My soul lies down among lions,
among people who are ablaze —
whose teeth are spears and arrows,
whose tongue is a sharpened sword.
verses.expanded_rendering: David describes his position with unflinching honesty. His nefesh ('soul, life') is be-tokh leva'im ('among lions') — he sleeps (eshkevah, 'I lie down') surrounded by predators. The bene adam ('children of humanity') are lohatim ('ablaze, burning') — consumed by hostile intent. Their weapons are dental and lingual: shinnehem chanit ve-chitsim ('their teeth are spears and arrows') and leshonam cherev chaddah ('their tongue is a sharpened sword'). The body-as-weapon catalogue is complete: teeth for close combat (spears) and ranged attack (arrows), tongue for cutting (sword). David's enemies are walking armories, and every weapon is part of their own anatomy.
translator_notes: The lion image transforms David's cave setting: he is not merely hiding in a cave — he is lying down in a lions' den. The cave that should be refuge has become a trap with predators. The tongue-as-sword image continues the thread from Psalms 52 and 55: speech is the deadliest weapon in these psalms.

verses.text_hebrew: ר֣וּמָה עַל־הַשָּׁמַ֣יִם אֱלֹהִ֑ים עַ֖ל כׇּל־הָאָ֣רֶץ כְּבוֹדֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens; let thy glory be above all the earth.
verses.rendering:
Be exalted above the heavens, God!
Let your glory be over all the earth!
translator_notes: This refrain appears again at verse 12, framing the psalm's second half. It reappears in Psalm 108:6. The prayer for God's glory over 'all the earth' expands the psalm's scope from personal rescue to cosmic reality — David's situation is a small piece of a much larger story.

verses.text_hebrew: רֶ֤שֶׁת ׀ הֵכִ֣ינוּ לִפְעָמַי֮ כָּפַ֢ף נַ֫פְשִׁ֥י כָּר֣וּ לְפָנַ֣י שִׁיחָ֑ה נָפְל֖וּ בְתוֹכָ֣הּ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: They have prepared a net for my steps; my soul is bowed down: they have digged a pit before me, into the midst whereof they are fallen themselves. Selah.
verses.rendering:
They set a net for my steps;
my soul was bowed low.
They dug a pit before me —
they fell into it themselves!
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The enemies' strategy is described in hunting terms: reshet ('net') for trapping and shichah ('pit') for capturing. But the psalm delivers poetic justice: naflu be-tokhah ('they fell into the middle of it'). The hunters become the hunted. The pit they dug for David swallowed them instead. This reversal — the trap catching the trapper — is a core principle of divine justice in the Hebrew Bible (Proverbs 26:27, Esther 7:10, Daniel 6:24). The verb kafaf ('bowed down, bent over') describes David's soul pressed low under the weight of persecution — but the bowing is temporary, while the enemies' fall into their own pit is final.
translator_notes: The selah after the reversal creates a pause for the listener to absorb the justice: they dug a pit, and they fell into it. The simplicity of the reversal is its power. No elaborate mechanism is needed — evil defeats itself when God is at work.

verses.text_hebrew: נָכ֣וֹן לִ֭בִּי אֱלֹהִ֣ים נָכ֣וֹן לִבִּ֑י אָשִׁ֖ירָה וַאֲזַמֵּֽרָה׃
verses.text_kjv: My heart is fixed, O God, my heart is fixed: I will sing and give praise.
verses.expanded_rendering: nakhon libbi ('my heart is steady/established') — the same word nakhon used for the 'steadfast spirit' David asked for in Psalm 51:12. Here David has it: a heart that is firm, established, not wobbling between fear and faith. The doubling — nakhon libbi, nakhon libbi — is emphatic and triumphant. From the man whose heart was writhing in Psalm 55:4, we now hear a heart that is fixed. And from that settled heart, two declarations: ashirah ('I will sing') and azammerah ('I will make music'). Song emerges from stability. The cave has become a concert hall.
translator_notes: nakhon from kun ('to establish, to make firm') is the opposite of mot ('to totter, to shake'). David's heart has stopped shaking. This does not mean his circumstances have changed — he is still in the cave, still hunted. But his inner condition has been established by trust.

verses.text_hebrew: ע֤וּרָה כְבוֹדִ֗י ע֭וּרָֽה הַנֵּ֥בֶל וְכִנּ֗וֹר אָעִ֥ירָה שָּֽׁחַר׃
verses.text_kjv: Awake up, my glory; awake, psaltery and harp: I myself will awake early.
verses.rendering:
Wake up, my glory!
Wake up, harp and lyre!
I will wake the dawn.
verses.expanded_rendering: David addresses his own kavod ('glory' — possibly his tongue, his honor, or his whole praising self) and his instruments (nevel, 'harp,' and kinnor, 'lyre') with the command urah ('wake up!'). Everything that can make music must rouse itself. Then the astonishing finale: a'irah shachar ('I will wake the dawn'). David does not say 'I will rise at dawn' — he says he will wake the dawn up. The dawn is sleeping, and David's praise will rouse it. This is not waiting for hope to arrive; it is summoning hope by singing before the light comes. In the darkness of the cave, David's music precedes the sunrise.
translator_notes: The phrase a'irah shachar ('I will wake the dawn') has been one of the most beloved lines in the Psalter across traditions. It reverses the normal relationship between the singer and the morning: usually dawn wakes the sleeper. Here the worshiper wakes the dawn. Praise creates the conditions for a new day.

verses.text_kjv: I will praise thee, O Lord, among the people: I will sing unto thee among the nations.
verses.rendering:
I will give thanks to you among the peoples, Lord;
I will make music to you among the nations.
translator_notes: The scope expands from David alone in a cave to thanksgiving ba-ammim ('among the peoples') and ba-le'ummim ('among the nations'). David's personal praise has universal ambition — what God has done for one fugitive in a cave deserves to be proclaimed to every nation. This verse is quoted in Romans 15:9 as evidence that God's salvation was always intended to reach the Gentiles.

verses.text_kjv: For thy mercy is great unto the heavens, and thy truth unto the clouds.
verses.rendering:
For your faithful love is great — reaching to the heavens,
and your truth to the skies.
verses.expanded_rendering: The paired attributes from verse 4 return, now given cosmic scale. chesed reaches ad shamayim ('to the heavens') and emet reaches ad shechaqim ('to the clouds/skies'). shechaqim (literally 'thin layers,' referring to the thin cloud layer of the upper sky) is even higher than shamayim in some poetic usages. David, from the lowest point he has ever been — the floor of a cave — looks up and sees God's love and truth extending all the way to the highest point that exists. The vertical distance between the cave floor and the upper atmosphere is the measure of God's character.
translator_notes: The pairing of chesed and emet with heavens and clouds creates a visual image: God's faithful love fills the entire vertical space between earth and the top of the sky. There is nowhere you can be — even in a cave — that God's love has not already reached.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: Here chesed is given spatial magnitude — it is gadol ('great') and reaches the heavens. The claim is not metaphorical but a statement about the scope of God's commitment: it extends to the farthest boundary imaginable.

verses.text_hebrew: ר֣וּמָה עַל־שָׁמַ֣יִם אֱלֹהִ֑ים עַ֖ל כׇּל־הָאָ֣רֶץ כְּבוֹדֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens: let thy glory be above all the earth.
verses.rendering:
Be exalted above the heavens, God!
Let your glory be over all the earth!
translator_notes: The refrain returns unchanged, closing the psalm with the same cosmic vision that punctuated its center. The repetition is liturgical — the congregation would have known this line and joined in. The cave is forgotten; the earth is covered in glory.
